did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses machine learning and deep learning methods, specifically a Two-Stage Hierarchical Classification model utilizing transfer learning with Convolutional Neural Networks (VGG16, DenseNet121, and custom CNNs), to classify skin lesions and diagnose skin cancers using the ISIC 2019 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a 'Two-Stage Hierarchical Classification model' using transfer learning (VGG16, DenseNet121, custom CNNs) on the ISIC 2019 dataset. It includes specific methodology, software environment details, dataset preprocessing, and quantitative results (accuracy, F1 scores) from experiments, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Two-Stage Hierarchical Skin Lesion Classification Using Transfer Learning
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of eight types of skin lesions and cancers using a Two-Stage Hierarchical Classification model based on transfer learning with VGG16, DenseNet121, and custom CNNs. The researchers utilized the ISIC 2019 dataset, merging it with other sources to create a balanced dataset of 42,291 images after data augmentation and undersampling. The model was validated using 3-fold stratified cross-validation to mitigate class imbalance issues present in previous works. On a semi-balanced dataset, the hierarchical model achieved an accuracy and weighted F1 score of 79%, while on a fully balanced dataset, it achieved 76% accuracy and 76% weighted F1 score. The second stage of the hierarchy specifically improved the distinction between Melanoma and Nevus, as well as Actinic Keratosis and Squamous Cell Carcinoma, with stage 2 models reaching up to 93% accuracy for specific sub-classifications. The authors conclude that this hierarchical approach provides more reliable performance metrics, particularly the F1 score, compared to related work by reducing bias from class imbalances.

all_task_types.target: Eight distinct skin lesion classes (Melanoma, Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesion, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis) via a Two-Stage Hierarchical approach.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions. Stage 1 classifies into six broader categories (including merged classes MEL/NV and AK/SCC), and Stage 2 performs binary classification for the merged classes (MEL vs NV, AK vs SCC). The task is explicitly defined as 'classification' with discrete diagnostic categories as targets, evaluated using accuracy, F1 score, precision, and recall.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: The primary dataset used in the study, comprising 25,331 images categorized into eight distinct types of skin lesions. It was sourced from the ISIC Archive.

use.training: Used as the primary source for the merged dataset. Images were augmented and undersampled to create semi-balanced and fully balanced subsets for training the Two-Stage Hierarchical Classification model.
use.validation: Used for validation via stratified 3-fold cross-validation during model training.
use.testing: Used for testing via stratified 3-fold cross-validation.
use.other: Served as the base for merging with other datasets to increase diversity and address class imbalance.

datasets_used.name: Merged Dataset (ISIC Archive + External Sources)
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset created by merging the ISIC 2019 dataset with other ISIC Challenge datasets (2016, 2017, 2018, 2020, 2024), the HAM10000 dataset, and the PROVe-AI dataset to increase diversity and underrepresented classes. After merging, augmentation, and undersampling, it resulted in a semi-balanced dataset of 42,291 images.

use.training: Used to train the Two-Stage Hierarchical Classification model (HCA-SBD) and single-stage classifiers. Specifically, a semi-balanced version was used for HCA-SBD, and a fully balanced version (3,500 images per class) was used for HCA-BD.
use.validation: Used for validation via stratified 3-fold cross-validation.
use.testing: Used for testing via stratified 3-fold cross-validation.
use.other: Created to mitigate class imbalance issues present in the original ISIC 2019 dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Challenge Datasets (2016, 2017, 2018, 2020, 2024)
datasets_used.description: Historical ISIC Challenge datasets sourced from the ISIC Archive, used to supplement the ISIC 2019 dataset.
classes_used: Various skin lesion classes (merged into the 8 primary classes)

use.training: Merged with ISIC 2019, HAM10000, and PROVe-AI to form the larger merged dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: The HAM10000 dataset, a large collection of dermatoscopic images of pigmented lesions, sourced from the ISIC Archive.
classes_used: Various skin lesion classes (merged into the 8 primary classes)

use.training: Merged with ISIC 2019 and other datasets to increase dataset size and diversity.

datasets_used.name: PROVe-AI Dataset
datasets_used.description: The PROVe-AI dataset, sourced from the ISIC Archive, used to supplement the primary dataset.
classes_used: Various skin lesion classes (merged into the 8 primary classes)

use.training: Merged with ISIC 2019 and other datasets to increase dataset size and diversity.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to validate the training and testing of the hierarchical classification models (HCA-SBD and HCA-BD) and single-stage classifiers to mitigate class imbalance and ensure robust performance estimation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, weighted F1 score, precision, and recall via stratified 3-fold cross-validation.

SC-SBD (Single Stage Classifier - Semi-B.dataset_description: Semi-balanced dataset derived from merged ISIC datasets (42,291 images total after undersampling NV, MEL, BCC to 6,500 each). Split into 80% training/validation and 20% testing via stratified sampling.
SC-SBD (Single Stage Classifier - Semi-B.notes: The ensemble model yielded the best performance among single-stage classifiers. Confusion was observed between MEL/NV and AK/SCC classes.

HCA-SBD (Two-Stage Hierarchical Classifi.dataset_description: Semi-balanced dataset used for hierarchical classification. Stage 1 trained on 6 classes (VASC, DF, BKL, BCC, MEL/NV, AK/SCC). Stage 2 trained on binary subsets.
HCA-SBD (Two-Stage Hierarchical Classifi.notes: Stage 1 classified into 6 classes. Stage 2 resolved confusion between MEL/NV and AK/SCC. The final hierarchical model showed an 8% increase in performance metrics compared to the non-hierarchical ensemble model (SC-SBD).

HCA-BD (Two-Stage Hierarchical Classifie.dataset_description: Fully balanced dataset created by undersampling to the lowest class count (3,500 images per class). Stage 1 classes: AK/SCC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL/NV, VASC (3,500 each). Stage 2 classes: MEL/NV (1,750 MEL, 1,750 NV), AK/SCC (1,750 AK, 1,750 SCC).
HCA-BD (Two-Stage Hierarchical Classifie.notes: The final model achieved 76% accuracy and weighted F1 score. The paper notes that the hierarchical approach provides more reliable performance metrics, particularly the F1 score, compared to related work by reducing bias from class imbalances.

architecture_used.main_model: Two-Stage Hierarchical Classification model using an ensemble of custom CNN, VGG16, and DenseNet121
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning with pre-trained VGG16 and DenseNet121 models; custom CNN trained from scratch

custom_cnn.layers: Three convolutional layers with increasing filters (32, 64, 128), followed by Maxpooling, ReLU activation, a dense layer with 256 neurons, and 50% dropout
custom_cnn.activation: ReLU for hidden layers, Softmax for final classification layer

vgg16.fine_tuning: Last four layers unfrozen for fine-tuning after initial feature extraction with frozen base layers
vgg16.classification_head: Flattening layer, 512-node dense layer with ReLU activation, 50% dropout layer, and dense layer with softmax activation

densenet121.fine_tuning: Last 60 layers unfrozen for fine-tuning after initial feature extraction with frozen base layers
densenet121.classification_head: Global average pooling, 512-neuron dense layer with ReLU activation, 50% dropout layer, and softmax layer

architecture_details.ensemble_method: Soft voting (averaging prediction probabilities) for combining CNN, VGG16, and DenseNet121 predictions in both Stage 1 and Stage 2

training_details.learning_rate: Decreased learning rate used during fine-tuning phase
training_details.epochs: Up to 50 epochs for feature extraction phase and 20 epochs for fine-tuning phase
training_details.early_stopping: Monitored validation loss, halted training after five epochs of no improvement, reloaded best-performing weights
training_details.data_split: 80% for training/validation (with 10% of that 80% used for validation) and 20% for testing

classification_or_output_head.stage_1: 6-class classification (VASC, DF, BKL, BCC, MEL/NV, AK/SCC) using ensemble of CNN, VGG16, DenseNet121
classification_or_output_head.stage_2_mel_nv: Binary classification (MEL vs NV) using ensemble of CNN, VGG16, DenseNet121
classification_or_output_head.stage_2_ak_scc: Binary classification (AK vs SCC) using ensemble of CNN, VGG16, DenseNet121
classification_or_output_head.final_output: Concatenated predictions from Stage 1 and Stage 2

preprocessing_techniques: Merging multiple datasets (ISIC Challenge 2016-2024, HAM10000, PROVe-AI) to increase diversity and sample size
Relabeling sub-classes (solar lentigo, seborrheic keratosis, pigmented benign keratosis) as Benign Keratosis (BKL)
Data augmentation for underrepresented classes (VASC, BKL, DF, AK, SCC) including random rotations (30° to 285°), horizontal and vertical flips, and brightness/contrast adjustments (90% to 110%)
Undersampling overrepresented classes (NV, MEL, BCC) to 6,500 images each for semi-balanced dataset creation
Undersampling all classes to 3,500 images each for fully balanced dataset creation
Stratified 3-fold cross-validation for data splitting and validation

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to underrepresented classes (VASC, BKL, DF, AK, SCC) with angles ranging from 30° to 285°.
Applied to underrepresented classes (VASC, BKL, DF, AK, SCC) to introduce spatial variability.
Controlled adjustments applied to underrepresented classes, ranging from 90% to 110% of the original values.

Used for class balancing augmentation. Overrepresented classes (NV, MEL, BCC) were undersampled to 6,500 images each for the semi-balanced dataset, and all classes were undersampled to 3,500 images each for the fully balanced dataset.

conclusions: The Two-Stage Hierarchical Classification model utilizing transfer learning (VGG16, DenseNet121, and custom CNNs) achieved an accuracy and weighted F1 score of 79% on a semi-balanced dataset and 76% on a fully balanced dataset.

The hierarchical approach effectively mitigated class imbalance issues present in the original ISIC 2019 dataset by merging datasets and using undersampling, resulting in more reliable performance metrics (particularly F1 score) compared to related work.

The second stage of the hierarchical model significantly improved the distinction between clinically similar lesions, specifically Melanoma vs. Nevus (92% accuracy on semi-balanced data) and Actinic Keratosis vs. Squamous Cell Carcinoma (93% accuracy on semi-balanced data).

The hierarchical model outperformed single-stage classifiers, showing an 8% increase in performance metrics compared to the best single-stage ensemble model (71% accuracy) on the semi-balanced dataset.

The study demonstrates that early detection of skin cancer can be aided by machine learning algorithms, potentially benefiting resource-constrained medical staff and patients by providing accurate diagnoses without immediate medical examination.
important_remarks: The study relies on a merged dataset created by combining ISIC 2019 with ISIC Challenge datasets (2016-2024), HAM10000, and PROVe-AI, which may introduce distribution shifts or biases not present in the original ISIC 2019 dataset.

Class imbalance was addressed via undersampling (NV, MEL, BCC reduced to 6,500 for semi-balanced; all classes to 3,500 for balanced) rather than oversampling, which discards potentially valuable data from overrepresented classes.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals to support the significance of performance improvements over related work.
External validation on independent, unseen clinical datasets is not performed; validation is limited to stratified 3-fold cross-validation on the merged dataset.

Data augmentation techniques (rotations, flips, brightness/contrast adjustments) were applied only to underrepresented classes (VASC, BKL, DF, AK, SCC), which may create uneven augmentation biases across classes.

The hierarchical model's Stage 2 performance (92-93% accuracy) is evaluated on a subset of data derived from Stage 1 predictions, which may not reflect real-world performance where Stage 1 errors propagate to Stage 2.

The paper critiques related work for class imbalance and lack of confusion matrices but does not provide a detailed analysis of its own model's failure cases or error distribution beyond the reported confusion matrices.

The study is a conference paper with limited space, potentially omitting detailed ablation studies, hyperparameter sensitivity analyses, or computational cost metrics (e.g., inference time, FLOPs) relevant for clinical deployment.
